part_subtitle: Larval Instars, Diagnostic Characteristics, Behavior, and Applied Importance

items.text: Chapter Six
Morphological Description of Tuta absoluta
Introduction

The larva represents the damaging stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. It is the only stage that feeds directly on tomato plant tissues and forms mines within leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva represents the damaging stage in the life cycle of Tuta absoluta. It is the only stage that feeds directly on tomato plant tissues and forms [[term:mines]]mines[[/term]] within leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits.

items.text: The egg, pupa, and adult moth do not cause direct mining damage. The egg represents the stage of embryonic development, the pupa is the transformation stage, while the adult moth is responsible for mating, dispersal, and egg laying.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The egg, [[term:pupation]]pupa[[/term]], and adult moth do not cause direct mining damage. The egg represents the stage of embryonic development, the pupa is the transformation stage, while the adult moth is responsible for mating, dispersal, and egg laying.

items.text: The larva passes through four successive instars:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva passes through four successive instars:

items.text: These instars are separated by three molts. At the end of the fourth instar, the larva enters the prepupal stage, stops feeding, and searches for a suitable site in which to pupate.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These instars are separated by three molts. At the end of the fourth instar, the larva enters the prepupal stage, stops feeding, and searches for a suitable site in which to pupate.

items.text: The four instars differ in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The four instars differ in:

items: Ability to move between plant parts.
Depth of position within plant tissues.
Level of protection from insecticides and natural enemies.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Level of protection from insecticides and [[term:biological-control]]natural enemies[[/term]].

items.text: Newly hatched larvae consume less tissue, but they establish the infestation by entering the plant. Advanced larvae consume greater quantities, form wider mines, and are more capable of attacking growing points, stems, and fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Newly hatched larvae consume less tissue, but they establish the infestation by entering the plant. Advanced larvae consume greater quantities, form wider mines, and are more capable of attacking growing points, stems, and fruits.

items.text: General Appearance of the Larva
The larva of Tuta absoluta is a lepidopteran larva with an elongated cylindrical body divided into relatively distinct segments.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva of Tuta absoluta is a lepidopteran larva with an elongated cylindrical body divided into relatively distinct segments.

items.text: The body consists of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The body consists of:

items: Abdomen composed of approximately ten visible segments.
Three pairs of true thoracic legs.
Fleshy abdominal prolegs that assist movement.
Fine setae distributed over the body.
Small cuticular plates or areas bearing the setae.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Abdomen composed of approximately ten visible segments.
[[prepared]]Small cuticular plates or areas bearing the setae.

items.text: The head is characterized by being:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The head is characterized by being:

items: Distinct and separated from the body.
Increasing in size as the larva passes from one instar to the next.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Increasing in size as the larva passes from one instar to the next.

items.text: The hardened layer covering the head is known as the:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The hardened layer covering the head is known as the:

items.text: Head Capsule

Head-capsule width is one of the most accurate indicators used to distinguish larval instars because body length changes temporarily as the larva contracts or extends, whereas head measurement is more stable within a given instar.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Head-capsule width is one of the most accurate indicators used to distinguish larval instars because body length changes temporarily as the larva contracts or extends, whereas head measurement is more stable within a given instar.

items.text: Parts of the Larval Body
Head
The head bears:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The head bears:

items: Strong chewing mandibles relative to larval size.
Mouthparts adapted for biting plant tissues.
Sensory setae that assist in selecting feeding sites.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Strong chewing mandibles relative to larval size.
[[prepared]]Sensory setae that assist in selecting feeding sites.

items.text: The larva uses its mandibles to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva uses its mandibles to:

items: Penetrate growing points, stems, and fruits.
Open an exit route from the mine or move to another site.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Open an exit route from the mine or move to another site.

items.text: The head is distinctly dark in the first instar, then ranges from brown to dark brown or partially black in advanced instars.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The head is distinctly dark in the first instar, then ranges from brown to dark brown or partially black in advanced instars.

items.text: Thorax
The thorax consists of three segments, each bearing one pair of true legs, giving a total of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The thorax consists of three segments, each bearing one pair of true legs, giving a total of:

items.text: Three pairs — six true legs.
On the first thoracic segment, immediately behind the head, there is a sclerotized plate known as the:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]On the first thoracic segment, immediately behind the head, there is a sclerotized plate known as the:

items.text: Prothoracic Shield
The color pattern of this shield is an important diagnostic feature in advanced larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The color pattern of this shield is an important diagnostic feature in advanced larvae.

items.text: In Tuta absoluta, the shield is relatively pale, with a dark shading or band along its posterior margin. This band becomes more distinct in advanced instars, whereas it may be absent or weak in very young larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In Tuta absoluta, the shield is relatively pale, with a dark shading or band along its posterior margin. This band becomes more distinct in advanced instars, whereas it may be absent or weak in very young larvae.

items.text: Abdomen
The abdomen consists of successive segments, some of which bear fleshy abdominal prolegs.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The abdomen consists of successive segments, some of which bear fleshy abdominal prolegs.

items.text: The abdominal prolegs do not resemble the jointed thoracic legs, but they help the larva to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The abdominal prolegs do not resemble the jointed thoracic legs, but they help the larva to:

items.text: The body contains lateral respiratory openings known as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The body contains lateral respiratory openings known as:

items.text: Spiracles
These permit air to enter the tracheal system.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These permit air to enter the tracheal system.

items.text: Setae and Seta-Bearing Areas
Fine sensory setae occur over the larval body and arise from small cuticular areas known as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fine sensory setae occur over the larval body and arise from small cuticular areas known as:

items.text: Pinacula
These setae help the larva detect:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These setae help the larva detect:

items.text: These characteristics are not easy to observe during field inspection, but they are used in microscopic diagnosis and in comparisons between Tuta absoluta and closely related species.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These characteristics are not easy to observe during field inspection, but they are used in microscopic diagnosis and in comparisons between Tuta absoluta and closely related species.

items.text: Number of Larval Instars
Tuta absoluta usually passes through four distinct larval instars.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta usually passes through four distinct larval instars.

items.text: The larva moves from one instar to the next through molting, during which:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva moves from one instar to the next through molting, during which:

items: 2. The old epidermis separates from the new epidermis.
4. The larva emerges with a new, soft covering.
5. The body expands before the new covering hardens.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]2. The old epidermis separates from the new epidermis.
[[prepared]]4. The larva emerges with a new, soft covering.
[[prepared]]5. The body expands before the new covering hardens.

items.text: Three molts occur during the larval stage:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Three molts occur during the larval stage:

items: First molt between L1 and L2.
Second molt between L2 and L3.
Third molt between L3 and L4.

items.text: After completing L4, the larva does not enter a fifth instar; instead, it transforms into the prepupal stage and then the pupa.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]After completing L4, the larva does not enter a fifth instar; instead, it transforms into the prepupal stage and then the pupa.

items.text: Instars are accurately determined using:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Instars are accurately determined using:

items: Body size and color as supporting indicators.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Body size and color as supporting indicators.

items.text: Body length alone is not a decisive criterion because it is affected by movement, feeding, preservation method, environmental conditions, and host plant.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Body length alone is not a decisive criterion because it is affected by movement, feeding, preservation method, environmental conditions, and [[term:host-plant]]host plant[[/term]].

items.text: First Larval Instar, L1
Appearance and Color
The first-instar larva emerges from the egg as a small and delicate larva with a body that is:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The first-instar larva emerges from the egg as a small and delicate larva with a body that is:

items: Provided with a distinct dark-brown or black head.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Provided with a distinct dark-brown or black head.

items.text: After feeding begins, the contents of the digestive tract may be visible through the translucent body.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]After feeding begins, the contents of the digestive tract may be visible through the translucent body.

items.text: The dark band behind the head is not always visible in this instar. Therefore, its absence in a newly hatched larva does not exclude Tuta absoluta.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The dark band behind the head is not always visible in this instar. Therefore, its absence in a newly hatched larva does not exclude Tuta absoluta.

items.text: Measurements
Published measurements vary according to the study and the method used to collect larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Published measurements vary according to the study and the method used to collect larvae.

items.text: The length of the first instar in different studies ranges approximately between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The length of the first instar in different studies ranges approximately between:

items.text: 0.6 and 1.5 mm.
Head-capsule width usually ranges around:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Head-capsule width usually ranges around:

items.text: One study recorded a range of 0.16–0.22 mm and a mean of 0.18 mm.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One study recorded a range of 0.16–0.22 mm and a mean of 0.18 mm.

items.text: The relatively wide variation in body length results from the fact that some measurements are taken immediately after hatching, whereas others are taken near the end of the first instar.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The relatively wide variation in body length results from the fact that some measurements are taken immediately after hatching, whereas others are taken near the end of the first instar.

items.text: Behavior After Hatching
After emerging from the egg, the larva begins searching for a suitable point at which to penetrate the plant.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]After emerging from the egg, the larva begins searching for a suitable point at which to penetrate the plant.

items.text: It may move over the leaf surface for a short period before biting through the epidermis and entering the tissue.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may move over the leaf surface for a short period before biting through the epidermis and entering the tissue.

items.text: Preferred sites include:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Preferred sites include:

items: At the bases of plant trichomes.
Within folds of the leaf surface.
Areas near the calyx or growing points.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Areas near the calyx or growing points.

items.text: The period before the larva enters the tissue is one of the most vulnerable periods in its life because it is exposed on the plant surface and is not yet protected within a mine.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The period before the larva enters the tissue is one of the most vulnerable periods in its life because it is exposed on the plant surface and is not yet protected within a mine.

items.text: Once entry is completed, the larva becomes positioned between the two epidermal layers of the leaf and begins consuming internal tissue.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Once entry is completed, the larva becomes positioned between the two epidermal layers of the leaf and begins consuming internal tissue.

items.text: Nature of Damage
The newly hatched larva forms a small and delicate mine that may appear as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The newly hatched larva forms a small and delicate mine that may appear as:

items: A mining point that is difficult to detect.
An area containing a small amount of frass.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A mining point that is difficult to detect.
[[prepared]]An area containing a small amount of [[term:frass]]frass[[/term]].

items.text: Damage caused by each individual larva is initially limited, but the importance of L1 lies in establishing the infestation site, which expands as the larva grows.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Damage caused by each individual larva is initially limited, but the importance of L1 lies in establishing the infestation site, which expands as the larva grows.

items.text: Duration of the First Instar
In one laboratory study, the duration of the first instar ranged between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In one laboratory study, the duration of the first instar ranged between:

items.text: Two and three days
With a mean of approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]With a mean of approximately:

items.text: 2.08 days.
However, this period varies according to temperature, host, cultivar, humidity, and the nutritional condition of the larva.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, this period varies according to temperature, host, cultivar, humidity, and the nutritional condition of the larva.

items.text: Importance in Control
The first instar is one of the most important control targets because:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The first instar is one of the most important control targets because:

items: It remains exposed for a short period after hatching.
It has not yet consumed a large quantity of tissue.
It is generally located close to the leaf surface.
It requires less food to ingest a lethal dose of some ingestion-active products.
It has a lower capacity to move between plant parts.
It generally has not yet entered fruits or deep stem tissues.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]It remains exposed for a short period after hatching.
[[prepared]]It has not yet consumed a large quantity of tissue.
[[prepared]]It is generally located close to the leaf surface.
[[prepared]]It requires less food to ingest a lethal dose of some ingestion-active products.
[[prepared]]It has a lower capacity to move between plant parts.
[[prepared]]It generally has not yet entered fruits or deep stem tissues.

items.text: However, the window for targeting this instar is short because the larva enters plant tissues rapidly after hatching.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, the window for targeting this instar is short because the larva enters plant tissues rapidly after hatching.

items.text: Second Larval Instar, L2
Appearance and Color
After the first molt, the larva increases in size and the body becomes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]After the first molt, the larva increases in size and the body becomes:

items: Provided with a black or dark-brown head.
More clearly visible within the mine.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Provided with a black or dark-brown head.

items.text: Green coloration begins to appear as the larva feeds on plant tissues and the digestive tract fills with green plant material.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Green coloration begins to appear as the larva feeds on plant tissues and the digestive tract fills with green plant material.

items.text: Measurements
The length of the second instar in different studies ranges approximately between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The length of the second instar in different studies ranges approximately between:

items.text: 1.6 and 2.7 mm.
Head-capsule width is approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Head-capsule width is approximately:

items.text: One study recorded a range of 0.24–0.35 mm and a mean of 0.30 mm.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One study recorded a range of 0.24–0.35 mm and a mean of 0.30 mm.

items.text: The clear increase in head width allows L2 to be distinguished from L1 more reliably than by body color alone.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The clear increase in head width allows L2 to be distinguished from L1 more reliably than by body color alone.

items.text: Behavior and Feeding
Most second-instar larvae remain inside leaves, but they become more capable of:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Most second-instar larvae remain inside leaves, but they become more capable of:

items: Moving between parts of the leaflet.

items.text: Dark frass becomes more visible within the mine, and the larva is easier to observe when the leaf is held against the light.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dark frass becomes more visible within the mine, and the larva is easier to observe when the leaf is held against the light.

items.text: Duration of the Second Instar
In one study, the duration of the second instar ranged between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In one study, the duration of the second instar ranged between:

items.text: Two and four days
With a mean of approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]With a mean of approximately:

items.text: 2.75 days.
The period becomes longer under cool conditions and shorter under warm temperatures within the suitable range.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The period becomes longer under cool conditions and shorter under warm temperatures within the suitable range.

items.text: Importance in Control
The second instar remains a relatively suitable control target, particularly when larvae are located within superficial leaf mines.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The second instar remains a relatively suitable control target, particularly when larvae are located within superficial leaf mines.

items.text: However, its survival capacity improves compared with the first instar, and the quantity of tissue consumed increases.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, its survival capacity improves compared with the first instar, and the quantity of tissue consumed increases.

items.text: Control is more successful when the active ingredient reaches the mine or the larva is forced to ingest it during feeding, rather than relying only on contact with the outer leaf surface.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Control is more successful when the [[term:active-ingredient]]active ingredient[[/term]] reaches the mine or the larva is forced to ingest it during feeding, rather than relying only on contact with the outer leaf surface.

items.text: Third Larval Instar, L3
Appearance and Color
In the third instar, the body becomes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In the third instar, the body becomes:

items: Light or dark green depending on the food.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Light or dark green depending on the food.

items.text: The dark band along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield begins to become more visible.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The dark band along the posterior margin of the [[term:prothoracic-shield]]prothoracic shield[[/term]] begins to become more visible.

items.text: The body color may shift to pinkish or brownish shades when feeding inside fruits or when the larva approaches the end of its development.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The body color may shift to pinkish or brownish shades when feeding inside fruits or when the larva approaches the end of its development.

items.text: Measurements
The length of the third instar is generally approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The length of the third instar is generally approximately:

items.text: Head-capsule width ranges approximately between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Head-capsule width ranges approximately between:

items.text: One study recorded a range of 0.35–0.45 mm and a mean of 0.38 mm.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One study recorded a range of 0.35–0.45 mm and a mean of 0.38 mm.

items.text: Activity and Damage
The third instar becomes more active and consumes more food. It can:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The third instar becomes more active and consumes more food. It can:

items: Move from one leaflet to another.

items.text: The amount of frass inside mines increases and may appear as irregular dark aggregations.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The amount of frass inside mines increases and may appear as irregular dark aggregations.

items.text: Economic damage begins to increase clearly during this instar, particularly when larvae reach reproductive plant parts.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Economic damage begins to increase clearly during this instar, particularly when larvae reach reproductive plant parts.

items.text: Duration of the Third Instar
In one study, the duration of the third instar ranged between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In one study, the duration of the third instar ranged between:

items.text: Three and five days
With a mean of approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]With a mean of approximately:

items.text: 3.56 days.
The duration varies according to temperature, cultivar, host, and the type of tissue on which the larva feeds.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The duration varies according to temperature, cultivar, host, and the type of tissue on which the larva feeds.

items.text: Importance of the Third Instar in Control
Control of the third instar becomes more difficult than control of L1 and L2 because it:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Control of the third instar becomes more difficult than control of L1 and L2 because it:

items: Occurs within wider and deeper mines.
Can move into stems and fruits.
Consumes a larger amount of tissue.
May leave the leaf before treatment reaches it.
Has a larger body size and greater ability to tolerate inadequate doses.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]May leave the leaf before treatment reaches it.
[[prepared]]Has a larger body size and greater ability to tolerate inadequate doses.

items.text: This does not mean that control is impossible, but treatment success depends more heavily on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This does not mean that control is impossible, but treatment success depends more heavily on:

items: Ability of the insecticide to penetrate plant tissues.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Ability of the insecticide to penetrate plant tissues.

items.text: Fourth Larval Instar, L4
Appearance and Color
The fourth instar is the largest and most conspicuous larval instar.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The fourth instar is the largest and most conspicuous larval instar.

items.text: Body color ranges between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Body color ranges between:

items: Pinkish brown near the prepupal stage.

items.text: The head is dark brown, and the dark band along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield becomes more distinct.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The head is dark brown, and the dark band along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield becomes more distinct.

items.text: Pink bands or areas may appear on the dorsal surface of the body, particularly in fully grown larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pink bands or areas may appear on the dorsal surface of the body, particularly in fully grown larvae.

items.text: Measurements
The length of the fourth instar in several studies is approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The length of the fourth instar in several studies is approximately:

items.text: The fully grown larva may reach approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The fully grown larva may reach approximately:

items.text: In some guides and geographical strains.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In some guides and geographical strains.

items.text: Head-capsule width usually measures approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Head-capsule width usually measures approximately:

items.text: One study recorded a range of 0.53–0.63 mm and a mean of 0.58 mm.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One study recorded a range of 0.53–0.63 mm and a mean of 0.58 mm.

items.text: Body width in some measurements reaches approximately 1.1 mm.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Body width in some measurements reaches approximately 1.1 mm.

items.text: Feeding and Damage
The fourth instar consumes the largest amount of tissue compared with earlier instars and can cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The fourth instar consumes the largest amount of tissue compared with earlier instars and can cause:

items: Collapse of large areas of the leaf.
Increased probability of entry by rot pathogens.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Collapse of large areas of the leaf.
[[prepared]]Increased probability of entry by rot pathogens.

items.text: The larva may leave an old mine and move to a new location. Therefore, empty mines may be present while active infestation continues elsewhere.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva may leave an old mine and move to a new location. Therefore, empty mines may be present while [[term:infestation-status]]active infestation[[/term]] continues elsewhere.

items.text: Large larvae may also be observed hanging from silken threads during movement or while searching for a pupation site.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Large larvae may also be observed hanging from silken threads during movement or while searching for a pupation site.

items.text: Duration of the Fourth Instar
In one study, the duration of the fourth instar ranged between:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In one study, the duration of the fourth instar ranged between:

items.text: Three and five days
With a mean of approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]With a mean of approximately:

items.text: 3.86 days.
The final part of this instar includes the period in which the larva becomes fully grown and prepares to stop feeding.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The final part of this instar includes the period in which the larva becomes fully grown and prepares to stop feeding.

items.text: End of the Fourth Instar
When development is completed:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When development is completed:

items: The larva stops enlarging the mine.
Part of the digestive tract is emptied.
The body may become shorter and more robust.
The larva leaves the feeding site.
It searches for a protected pupation site.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Part of the digestive tract is emptied.
[[prepared]]The body may become shorter and more robust.
[[prepared]]It searches for a protected pupation site.

items.text: Pupation may occur within:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Pupation may occur within:

items: A mine or protected plant part.
A silken cocoon on the leaf.

items.text: Table of Larval Instar Measurements
Measurements vary according to strain, host, temperature, and exact stage at measurement. Therefore, the following values represent approximate practical ranges:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Measurements vary according to strain, host, temperature, and exact stage at measurement. Therefore, the following values represent approximate practical ranges:

rows: White or creamy with a dark head
Green to pink or pinkish brown
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]White or creamy with a dark head

items.text: One morphometric study recorded the following means:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One morphometric study recorded the following means:

items.text: A small difference in length alone should not be used to determine the instar; the head, cast skin, color, and behavior should be examined together.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A small difference in length alone should not be used to determine the instar; the head, cast skin, color, and behavior should be examined together.

items.text: Change in Larval Color
Larval color is not a fixed characteristic and is not sufficient by itself for diagnosis.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larval color is not a fixed characteristic and is not sufficient by itself for diagnosis.

items.text: Color is affected by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Color is affected by:

items: Exposure to an insecticide or pathogen.

items.text: The larva remains pale for several hours after molting because the new covering has not yet acquired its final color and hardness.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva remains pale for several hours after molting because the new covering has not yet acquired its final color and hardness.

items.text: Advanced larvae may become:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Advanced larvae may become:

items: Pink when the body is fully fed or approaching pupation.
Brown or dark when diseased or dead.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Pink when the body is fully fed or approaching pupation.
[[prepared]]Brown or dark when diseased or dead.

items.text: Therefore, observing a pink larva does not mean that it is a different species, and observing a green larva does not confirm that it is Tuta.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Therefore, observing a pink larva does not mean that it is a different species, and observing a green larva does not confirm that it is Tuta.

items.text: Dark Band Behind the Head
One of the most important characters used to identify Tuta absoluta larvae is the presence of a dark band along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield immediately behind the head.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]One of the most important characters used to identify Tuta absoluta larvae is the presence of a dark band along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield immediately behind the head.

items.text: This band appears as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]This band appears as:

items: A transverse band behind the head.

items.text: It is more distinct in the third and fourth instars, whereas it may be absent or weak in young larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It is more distinct in the third and fourth instars, whereas it may be absent or weak in young larvae.

items.text: However, this character is not definitive by itself because tomato pinworm:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, this character is not definitive by itself because tomato pinworm:

items.text: Keiferia lycopersicella
Also possesses a dark band behind the head, although it is generally less distinct.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Also possesses a dark band behind the head, although it is generally less distinct.

items.text: Reliable differentiation between the two species requires examination of additional characters and may involve:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Reliable differentiation between the two species requires examination of additional characters and may involve:

items.text: Molting and Head Capsules
During transition between instars, the larva leaves behind its old external covering. The mine may contain:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]During transition between instars, the larva leaves behind its old external covering. The mine may contain:

items: Frass accumulated near the molting site.

items.text: The old head capsule is dark and relatively hard and may be used to determine the previous instar.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The old head capsule is dark and relatively hard and may be used to determine the previous instar.

items.text: Finding more than one head capsule within a mine may indicate that the larva spent several instars in the same area, but does not necessarily mean that several larvae were present.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Finding more than one head capsule within a mine may indicate that the larva spent several instars in the same area, but does not necessarily mean that several larvae were present.

items.text: Larval movement and feeding temporarily decline before molting and resume after the new covering hardens.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larval movement and feeding temporarily decline before molting and resume after the new covering hardens.

items.text: Mining Behavior Within Leaves
The larva feeds between the two epidermal layers of the leaf, consuming mesophyll tissue while part of the outer epidermis remains intact.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva feeds between the two epidermal layers of the leaf, consuming mesophyll tissue while part of the outer epidermis remains intact.

items.text: The mine begins small and then expands as the larva grows, becoming:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The mine begins small and then expands as the larva grows, becoming:

items: A dry brown blotch in old infestation.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A dry brown blotch in old infestation.

items.text: Dark excreta known as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Dark excreta known as:

items.text: Frass
Are commonly present within the mine.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Are commonly present within the mine.

items.text: The frass may accumulate in one part of the mine rather than being distributed evenly.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The frass may accumulate in one part of the mine rather than being distributed evenly.

items.text: Tuta mines are generally broad and blotch-like, whereas mines formed by Liriomyza flies are usually narrower, winding, or serpentine.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta mines are generally broad and blotch-like, whereas mines formed by [[term:liriomyza]]Liriomyza[[/term]] flies are usually narrower, winding, or serpentine.

items.text: However, several overlapping Liriomyza mines may form a broad area. Therefore, the mine should be opened and the larva itself examined.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, several overlapping Liriomyza mines may form a broad area. Therefore, the mine should be opened and the larva itself examined.

items.text: Movement of Larvae Between Feeding Sites
The larva does not necessarily remain inside the first mine throughout the larval stage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva does not necessarily remain inside the first mine throughout the larval stage.

items.text: It may leave and move to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]It may leave and move to:

items: A new site within the same leaf.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]A new site within the same leaf.

items.text: The larva uses a silken thread to assist in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva uses a silken thread to assist in:

items.text: Large larvae may hang from the plant by a silken thread, particularly when plants are shaken or the feeding site is disturbed.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Large larvae may hang from the plant by a silken thread, particularly when plants are shaken or the feeding site is disturbed.

items.text: Infestation of Growing Points and Stems
When the larva enters the growing point or a tender stem, it may cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When the larva enters the growing point or a tender stem, it may cause:

items: Wilting of the plant part beyond the infestation site.
Reduced fruit set and early yield.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Wilting of the plant part beyond the infestation site.

items.text: Transplants and young plants are more sensitive to growing-point infestation because loss of the main apex represents a large proportion of the plant’s vegetative structure.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Transplants and young plants are more sensitive to growing-point infestation because loss of the main apex represents a large proportion of the plant’s vegetative structure.

items.text: Frass may be present at the entry hole or inside the infested bud.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Frass may be present at the entry hole or inside the infested bud.

items.text: Fruit Infestation
Larvae enter fruits from different sites. Entry is commonly found:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larvae enter fruits from different sites. Entry is commonly found:

items: At points where a leaf contacts the fruit.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]At points where a leaf contacts the fruit.

items.text: The larva leaves:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva leaves:

items.text: The size of the entry hole does not reflect the extent of internal damage. The hole may be minute while the tunnel extends through a large area of the fruit.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The size of the entry hole does not reflect the extent of internal damage. The hole may be minute while the tunnel extends through a large area of the fruit.

items.text: Fruit infestation is particularly serious because it converts partially compensable leaf damage into direct loss of marketable or processable yield.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fruit infestation is particularly serious because it converts partially compensable leaf damage into direct loss of marketable or processable yield.

items.text: Duration of the Larval Stage
The duration of the larval stage changes greatly according to temperature.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The duration of the larval stage changes greatly according to temperature.

items.text: In one study conducted under constant temperatures, the total duration of the larval stage was as follows:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In one study conducted under constant temperatures, the total duration of the larval stage was as follows:

items.text: These values demonstrate that cold conditions do not necessarily prevent feeding, but they prolong the period during which larvae remain on the plant.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]These values demonstrate that cold conditions do not necessarily prevent feeding, but they prolong the period during which larvae remain on the plant.

items.text: In other studies conducted under warm conditions, the total duration generally ranged around:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In other studies conducted under warm conditions, the total duration generally ranged around:

items.text: 10–14 days
And may extend for longer periods depending on temperature, cultivar, and host.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]And may extend for longer periods depending on temperature, cultivar, and host.

items.text: A shorter duration at high temperature does not always mean greater damage by an individual larva because daily food intake, survival, and physiological condition also change.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A shorter duration at high temperature does not always mean greater damage by an individual larva because daily food intake, survival, and physiological condition also change.

items.text: Most Suitable Temperatures for Larval Development
Development becomes faster as temperature increases within the suitable range, but survival does not continue to increase at extreme temperatures.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Development becomes faster as temperature increases within the suitable range, but survival does not continue to increase at extreme temperatures.

items.text: In one study:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]In one study:

items.text: Thermal studies indicate that a range of approximately:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Thermal studies indicate that a range of approximately:

items.text: Provides an appropriate balance among:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Provides an appropriate balance among:

items: Successful transformation to the pupal stage.

items.text: Temperatures around 30°C shorten the duration of the stage, but the overall effect depends on exposure duration, humidity, strain, and host plant.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Temperatures around 30°C shorten the duration of the stage, but the overall effect depends on exposure duration, humidity, strain, and host plant.

items.text: Extremely high or low temperatures cause:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Extremely high or low temperatures cause:

items: Development of abnormal larvae or pupae.

items.text: Effect of Host Plant and Cultivar
Larvae do not develop at the same rate on all plants or tomato cultivars.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larvae do not develop at the same rate on all plants or tomato cultivars.

items.text: Their performance is affected by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Their performance is affected by:

items.text: Studies on processing tomato cultivars recorded larval durations ranging from less than nine days on some cultivars to more than twelve days on others.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Studies on processing tomato cultivars recorded larval durations ranging from less than nine days on some cultivars to more than twelve days on others.

items.text: Larval duration alone is not sufficient to evaluate cultivar resistance. The following should also be measured:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larval duration alone is not sufficient to evaluate cultivar resistance. The following should also be measured:

items.text: Sensitivity of Larvae to Different Factors
Desiccation
Larvae inside mines are relatively protected from dry air because the leaf provides a more humid microclimate.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larvae inside mines are relatively protected from dry air because the leaf provides a more humid microclimate.

items.text: However, larvae become more exposed to desiccation when they:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]However, larvae become more exposed to desiccation when they:

items: Are newly hatched before penetrating the tissue.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Are newly hatched before penetrating the tissue.

items.text: High temperature and dry air increase water loss, especially in small larvae.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]High temperature and dry air increase water loss, especially in small larvae.

items.text: Rainfall and Overhead Irrigation
Water droplets do not easily reach larvae established inside mines, but they may affect exposed larvae by:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Water droplets do not easily reach larvae established inside mines, but they may affect exposed larvae by:

items: Increasing humidity and encouraging some entomopathogens.
Removing a proportion of newly hatched larvae.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Removing a proportion of newly hatched larvae.

items.text: Overhead irrigation or rainfall is not a sufficient control method because a large proportion of larvae remain protected within plant tissues.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Overhead irrigation or rainfall is not a sufficient control method because a large proportion of larvae remain protected within plant tissues.

items.text: Predators
Larvae, especially young larvae close to the mine surface, are preyed upon by organisms including:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larvae, especially young larvae close to the mine surface, are preyed upon by organisms including:

items.text: Predators may pierce the leaf epidermis and suck the larva located inside the mine or capture it while it is leaving and moving.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Predators may pierce the leaf epidermis and suck the larva located inside the mine or capture it while it is leaving and moving.

items.text: Larval Parasitoids
Different parasitoids attack Tuta larvae, including species belonging to groups such as:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Different parasitoids attack Tuta larvae, including species belonging to groups such as:

items.text: The suitable larval instar for parasitism varies according to parasitoid species.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The suitable larval instar for parasitism varies according to parasitoid species.

items.text: The parasitoid may lay its egg:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The parasitoid may lay its egg:

items: Beside the larva within the mine after paralyzing it.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Beside the larva within the mine after paralyzing it.

items.text: Excessive use of broad-spectrum insecticides kills these parasitoids and reduces their natural contribution.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Excessive use of broad-spectrum insecticides kills these parasitoids and reduces their natural contribution.

items.text: Entomopathogenic Bacteria
Products containing Bacillus thuringiensis act after the larva ingests the crystals and bacteria deposited on treated tissue.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Products containing Bacillus thuringiensis act after the larva ingests the crystals and bacteria deposited on treated tissue.

items.text: Young instars are generally more suitable targets because they:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Young instars are generally more suitable targets because they:

items: Have not yet entered fruits or deep stem tissues.
Are less capable of tolerating intestinal damage.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Have not yet entered fruits or deep stem tissues.
[[prepared]]Are less capable of tolerating intestinal damage.

items.text: Efficacy declines when:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Efficacy declines when:

items: The material does not reach the feeding site.
The product degrades under radiation and environmental conditions.
Resistance or reduced susceptibility is present.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]The material does not reach the feeding site.
[[prepared]]The product degrades under radiation and environmental conditions.

items.text: Entomopathogenic Fungi
Fungi such as Beauveria bassiana and Metarhizium anisopliae can infect larvae when conidia reach the body and suitable humidity permits germination.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Fungi such as Beauveria bassiana and Metarhizium anisopliae can infect larvae when conidia reach the body and suitable humidity permits germination.

items.text: Exposed larvae or larvae moving outside mines are more likely to come into contact with the fungi, whereas direct contact is less likely for larvae deep inside fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Exposed larvae or larvae moving outside mines are more likely to come into contact with the fungi, whereas direct contact is less likely for larvae deep inside fruits.

items.text: Entomopathogenic Nematodes
Some entomopathogenic nematodes can reach larvae inside mines when a film of moisture allows their movement.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Some entomopathogenic nematodes can reach larvae inside mines when a film of moisture allows their movement.

items.text: Their efficacy is associated with:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Their efficacy is associated with:

items.text: Chemical Insecticides
Larval susceptibility to insecticides varies according to:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larval susceptibility to insecticides varies according to:

items: Mode of action of the insecticide.
Ability of the material to penetrate the leaf.
History of use of the insecticide group in the field.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Ability of the material to penetrate the leaf.
[[prepared]]History of use of the insecticide group in the field.

items.text: Young instars are generally easier to target than large larvae located inside fruits and stems.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Young instars are generally easier to target than large larvae located inside fruits and stems.

items.text: Changing the trade name does not mean changing the mode of action because several products may belong to the same IRAC group.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Changing the trade name does not mean changing the mode of action because several products may belong to the same [[term:irac]]IRAC group[[/term]].

items.text: Why Are Advanced Larvae More Difficult to Control?
Control becomes more difficult from L1 to L4 for the following reasons:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Control becomes more difficult from L1 to L4 for the following reasons:

items: Larger body size and greater ability to tolerate an incomplete dose.
Increased infestation of fruits and stems.
Reduced direct exposure of the body to spray solution.
Ability to leave the treated mine.
Rapid production of damage before death.
Possible resistance to the active ingredient.
Reduced efficacy of some biological insecticides against large instars.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Larger body size and greater ability to tolerate an incomplete dose.
[[prepared]]Reduced direct exposure of the body to spray solution.
[[prepared]]Reduced efficacy of some biological insecticides against large instars.

items.text: Therefore, program efficacy is not measured only by its ability to kill L4, but by its ability to prevent a large proportion of the pest population from reaching that stage.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Therefore, program efficacy is not measured only by its ability to kill L4, but by its ability to prevent a large proportion of the pest population from reaching that stage.

items.text: Distinguishing Tuta Larvae from Liriomyza Larvae

rows: Not the usual form of damage

items.text: When there is uncertainty, the mine should be opened carefully. The presence of a distinct head and legs confirms that the larva is lepidopteran and not a leaf-mining fly larva.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When there is uncertainty, the mine should be opened carefully. The presence of a distinct head and legs confirms that the larva is lepidopteran and not a leaf-mining fly larva.

items.text: Distinguishing Tuta from Tomato Fruitworm
The larva of Tuta absoluta differs from Helicoverpa armigera in several characteristics:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva of Tuta absoluta differs from Helicoverpa armigera in several characteristics:

rows: Inside the mine or near the opening
Larger quantities near the fruit opening
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]Inside the mine or near the opening

items.text: Distinguishing Tuta absoluta from Tomato Pinworm
Tuta absoluta resembles tomato pinworm, Keiferia lycopersicella, in:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Tuta absoluta resembles tomato pinworm, Keiferia lycopersicella, in:

items: Presence of a dark band behind the head.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Presence of a dark band behind the head.

items.text: For this reason, reliable separation based on the larva or damage alone may be difficult.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]For this reason, reliable separation based on the larva or damage alone may be difficult.

items.text: The dark marking behind the head is usually stronger in Tuta, but final diagnosis in regions where both species occur may require specialist examination.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The dark marking behind the head is usually stronger in Tuta, but final diagnosis in regions where both species occur may require specialist examination.

items.text: Field Examination of Larvae
Field examination begins with recent mines rather than only with dry leaves.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Field examination begins with recent mines rather than only with dry leaves.

items.text: The following steps are used:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The following steps are used:

items: 1. Select plants from field edges and the interior.
4. Hold the leaf against the light to locate the larva.
5. Open the mine using a fine needle.
6. Record the presence or absence of a live larva.
8. Examine the head and prothoracic band.
10. Inspect growing points, stems, and fruits.
11. Do not count an old empty mine as active infestation.
12. Record the number of infested plants, not only the number of mines.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]1. Select plants from field edges and the interior.
[[prepared]]4. Hold the leaf against the light to locate the larva.
[[prepared]]5. Open the mine using a fine needle.
[[prepared]]6. Record the presence or absence of a live larva.
[[prepared]]8. Examine the head and prothoracic band.
[[prepared]]10. Inspect growing points, stems, and fruits.
[[prepared]]11. Do not count an old empty mine as active infestation.
[[prepared]]12. Record the number of infested plants, not only the number of mines.

items.text: Assessment should distinguish among:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Assessment should distinguish among:

items: New mines containing L1 or L2.
Wide active mines containing L3 or L4.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Wide active mines containing L3 or L4.

items.text: Collection and Preservation of Larvae
The larva should be collected together with the plant tissue in which it occurs because forceful removal may tear it.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva should be collected together with the plant tissue in which it occurs because forceful removal may tear it.

items.text: The collection method includes:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The collection method includes:

items: Placing it in a well-ventilated container.
Adding a fresh leaf when necessary.
Recording the location, date, and cultivar.
Separating fruit-infesting larvae from leaf samples.
Avoiding exposure of containers to sunlight.
Transferring samples rapidly to the examination site.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Transferring samples rapidly to the examination site.

items.text: When preserving a specimen for morphological diagnosis, an appropriate preservation method should be used to avoid loss of body color or deformation of the head capsule.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]When preserving a specimen for morphological diagnosis, an appropriate preservation method should be used to avoid loss of body color or deformation of the head capsule.

items.text: Applied Importance of Larval Instars
The four instars are not equal in their importance for decision-making.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The four instars are not equal in their importance for decision-making.

rows: Beginning of penetration or a small mine
Wide mines, growing points, stems, and fruits
Deep mines, fruits, and movement toward pupation
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]Beginning of penetration or a small mine
[[prepared]]Wide mines, growing points, stems, and fruits
[[prepared]]Deep mines, fruits, and movement toward pupation

items.text: A successful program focuses on:
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]A successful program focuses on:

items: Preventing hatching as much as possible.
Reducing the number of larvae reaching L3 and L4.
Removing sources of larvae and pupae at the end of the season.
prepared_items: [[prepared]]Reducing the number of larvae reaching L3 and L4.
[[prepared]]Removing sources of larvae and pupae at the end of the season.

items.text: Reference Table for the Larval Stage

rows: Yellowish green, bluish green, or pink
Approximately 7.2–7.6 mm and may reach 9–10 mm
Dark band behind the head in advanced instars
Leaves, growing points, stems, and fruits
Exit and search for a pupation site
prepared_rows: [[prepared]]Dark band behind the head in advanced instars
[[prepared]]Exit and search for a pupation site

items.text: Summary of the Larval Stage
The larva is the stage responsible for all direct damage caused by Tuta absoluta. It passes through four instars separated by three molts.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larva is the stage responsible for all direct damage caused by Tuta absoluta. It passes through four instars separated by three molts.

items.text: The first-instar larva emerges small, white or creamy, and with a dark head, then rapidly enters plant tissue. The body gradually increases in size and becomes green or pink during advanced instars.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The first-instar larva emerges small, white or creamy, and with a dark head, then rapidly enters plant tissue. The body gradually increases in size and becomes green or pink during advanced instars.

items.text: Approximate instar lengths range from less than one millimeter to approximately 7.5 mm, while the fully grown larva may reach approximately 9–10 mm. Head-capsule width is more accurate than body length for determining larval instar.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Approximate instar lengths range from less than one millimeter to approximately 7.5 mm, while the fully grown larva may reach approximately 9–10 mm. Head-capsule width is more accurate than body length for determining larval instar.

items.text: Advanced larvae are characterized by a dark band behind the head along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield, but this character alone is not sufficient to distinguish them from some closely related species.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Advanced larvae are characterized by a dark band behind the head along the posterior margin of the prothoracic shield, but this character alone is not sufficient to distinguish them from some closely related species.

items.text: Larvae begin by mining leaves and then become increasingly capable of attacking growing points, petioles, stems, and fruits. They form irregular blotch mines that commonly contain dark frass.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Larvae begin by mining leaves and then become increasingly capable of attacking growing points, petioles, stems, and fruits. They form irregular blotch mines that commonly contain dark frass.

items.text: The larval stage lasts approximately 10–14 days under suitable warm conditions but may extend beyond one month under cold conditions. The most suitable thermal range for larval development and survival is approximately 20–25°C.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]The larval stage lasts approximately 10–14 days under suitable warm conditions but may extend beyond one month under cold conditions. The most suitable thermal range for larval development and survival is approximately 20–25°C.

items.text: Control becomes more difficult as larval age advances because large larvae enter deeper tissues, cause greater damage, and become less exposed to insecticides and natural enemies.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Control becomes more difficult as larval age advances because large larvae enter deeper tissues, cause greater damage, and become less exposed to insecticides and natural enemies.

items.text: Effective management therefore focuses on detecting eggs and recent mines and targeting L1 and L2 before larvae reach advanced instars and fruits.
items.prepared_text: [[prepared]]Effective management therefore focuses on detecting eggs and recent mines and targeting L1 and L2 before larvae reach advanced instars and fruits.

meta.part_subtitle: Larval Instars, Diagnostic Characteristics, Behavior, and Applied Importance
